chunk_summary: Evaluation of growth velocity and pubertal tempo in school-age children and adolescents involves assessing height velocity, pubertal staging, and clinical context to identify potential growth disorders or delays.
learning_objectives: Understand the expected growth velocity in mid-childhood and its clinical significance.
Interpret pubertal staging and its impact on growth patterns and timing.
Identify clinical signs that warrant referral for growth failure evaluation.
Counsel on lifestyle factors that may influence growth and pubertal tempo.
key_facts: Typical height velocity in mid-childhood (3 years to puberty) is 5–6 cm/year.
Persistent growth velocity below 5 cm/year with downward centile crossing suggests pathology.
Pubertal staging is critical: early puberty may shorten growth potential, while delayed puberty can mimic growth failure.
Accurate re-measurement at least 6 months apart is essential to reduce measurement noise.
Improving diet, sleep, anemia status, and chronic illness control may enhance growth velocity.
reference_quote: In mid-childhood (about 3 years to puberty), typical height velocity is roughly 5–6 cm/year; persistent velocity clearly below this, especially with downward centile crossing, suggests pathology or sustained constraint that warrants evaluation. (Source: Reference 1)
document_key_topics: Growth velocity in mid-childhood and adolescence
Impact of pubertal timing on growth patterns
